div.div: Effect: A string loop is tied around your hand and wrist — seemingly inescapable. With one smooth motion, the loop slips off entirely while a spectator holds both ends. Method: as the string is being tied, secretly position it so that it crosses over a fold of skin at your thumb joint. This creates hidden slack in the loop. With a fluid pulling motion toward your fingertips, work this slack through the thumb crossing and the loop slides off cleanly. Practice the hand position until it's completely natural-looking.

Escape from bonds was one of the most requested feats of Victorian conjurers. Long before Houdini's famous escapes, lesser-known performers were building entire acts around rope and string escapes using clever mechanical advantages rather than brute force.

A string is tied in a loop around the magician's hand and wrist, seemingly inescapable. With a quick movement, the loop slips off entirely despite the spectator holding both ends. The secret lies in arranging the string over the fingers in a specific looped configuration that creates hidden slack.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
